questions.text: I feel most alive when experiencing something new or intense.
I start things excited but often lose interest before finishing them.
I'm drawn to experiences that are edgy or push my boundaries.
When life gets predictable, I feel restless.
I find comfort in familiar routines and things I know well.
When stressed, I reach for food, entertainment, or other pleasures.
I sometimes overdo things (eating, drinking, shopping) even when I know I should stop.
I think carefully about how I spend money and time on comfort/pleasure.
I dive fully into new things when I get excited about them.
I'm more energized by short bursts of intensity than by steady, sustained effort.
Boredom is one of the hardest things for me to tolerate.
Routine and predictability wear me down over time.
I notice beauty, aesthetics, and sensory details more than most people seem to.
I'm drawn toward activities that carry some element of risk or uncertainty.
I see humor in things and use humor to navigate life.
I can laugh at difficult situations without making others feel dismissed.
People would say I'm naturally lighthearted and fun to be around.
Humor helps me process hard things without avoiding them.
I'm aware of how others perceive me and it affects how I present myself.
I feel a genuine lift when I receive recognition from people who matter to me.
When I accomplish something, I want the right people to know about it.
My sense of how I'm doing in life is affected by feedback from people I respect.
I want acknowledgment from colleagues when I've contributed something meaningful.
When my work goes unrecognized by people I respect, it bothers me.
When someone I care about seems disappointed in me, I replay it and look for what I did wrong.
I say yes to people I care about even when the honest answer should be no.
I measure my progress against specific people whose lives I pay attention to.
When someone in my peer group succeeds, I notice my internal response before genuine happiness.
I tell people what I honestly see even if it might hurt the relationship.
I speak up in groups even when it creates friction with people whose opinion matters.
I feel genuinely energized by being part of something larger than myself.
I invest in the health of groups I belong to, even when it costs me.
When I feel rejected by someone who matters, it affects my mood for days.
When I sense someone pulling away, I work to re-establish connection.
I feel a pull toward productivity even during periods meant for rest.
I take genuine satisfaction in completing things and reaching milestones.
When I'm not making progress toward something meaningful, I feel dissatisfied.
I think regularly about what I'm building that will still matter after I move on.
I'm motivated to invest in people and things that will outlast my involvement.
I measure my work's significance by its lasting effect, not its immediate results.
I'm drawn to problems that are large enough to matter at a scale beyond my immediate context.
You led a project to success. Your team gets credit; your contribution isn't mentioned. Your honest feeling afterward:

options.text: The work succeeded — that's what matters.
Frustration that affects how I feel about the success.

questions.text: You invested deeply in mentoring someone. They succeed publicly without mentioning your role. Your honest response:

options.text: Pride — this is exactly what the investment was for.
A sense of loss I carry longer than I should.
Significant hurt — feels like a betrayal.

questions.text: You led a project that failed publicly. You were clearly responsible. In the weeks after:

options.text: I own it clearly and move forward on what I learned.
I own it publicly but privately revisit what others could have done.
I own my decisions but the public failure is much harder to carry.
I struggle to separate the failure from my own worth.

questions.text: Someone less experienced is placed in leadership above you. You believe it's wrong. Over the following months:

options.text: I commit to making them successful.
I do my job well while privately holding my assessment.
My motivation is genuinely affected, though I manage it.
I look for an exit — I can't sustain contribution here.

questions.text: I track how my progress compares to peers at similar life stages.
The success of people in my field spurs me to push harder.
I'm energized by contexts where performance is ranked or compared.
I feel most like myself when I'm setting direction for others.
I'm drawn to situations where the outcome depends on my ability to influence others.
It's difficult for me to contribute fully without being in a position to shape direction.
I struggle to release work I know could be better, even if no one would notice.
I'd rather be exceptional at a few things than competent at many.
I hold myself to a standard that most people would consider unnecessarily high.
